title: Cantonese-Style Chicken Chow Mein

ingredients.name: dried egg noodles (hakka or thin egg noodles)

subs.name: hakka noodles (widely available in India) or thin wheat noodles
subs.notes: instant noodles work in a pinch — discard the masala sachets

ingredients.name: chicken breast or thigh, thinly sliced

subs.name: 300 g tofu, pressed and sliced, for a vegetarian version
subs.notes: fry tofu until golden before adding

ingredients.name: spring onions, cut into 5 cm lengths

subs.name: extra 1 tbsp soy sauce + 1 tsp sugar
skip or use a drop of mustard oil for a nutty finish

steps.text: Marinate the sliced chicken with 1 teaspoon soy sauce, the cornflour and a pinch of salt. Set aside for 10 minutes.
Cook the noodles in boiling water for 1 minute less than the packet says — they should be just underdone. Drain, rinse with cold water and toss with 1 teaspoon of oil to prevent sticking.
Mix together the remaining soy sauce and the oyster sauce in a small bowl and set aside.

Heat a wok or wide kadai over the highest flame until smoking. Add 2 tablespoons of oil and stir-fry the chicken, spreading it in a single layer. Cook undisturbed for 1 minute until golden on one side, then toss and cook for another minute until just cooked through. Remove and set aside.
In the same wok, add the remaining oil. Add garlic and ginger, stir for 20 seconds. Add carrot and cabbage and stir-fry on high heat for 2 minutes until they soften slightly but retain some crunch.
Add the noodles, spreading them out. Let them sit on the heat for 30 seconds to pick up colour, then toss everything together.
Pour the sauce over the noodles, return the chicken, and add the spring onions and bean sprouts if using. Toss vigorously for 1 minute until everything is well coated and piping hot.
Remove from heat, drizzle with sesame oil, and serve immediately — chow mein waits for no one.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: oyster-sauce → extra 1 tbsp soy sauce + 1 tsp sugar (flavour will be less savoury-sweet); chicken → 300 g tofu, pressed and sliced, for a vegetarian version (fry tofu until golden before adding); noodles → hakka noodles (widely available in India) or thin wheat noodles (instant noodles work in a pinch — discard the masala sachets); gingelly-oil → skip or use a drop of mustard oil for a nutty finish
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Use widely available Chings hakka noodles or any dried egg noodles from Indian grocery stores. A large kadai on high gas flame works well. Oyster sauce and soy sauce are easily found in Indian supermarkets; the Chings and Maggi brands are reliable and affordable. Bean sprouts can be substituted with blanched moong sprouts (ankurit moong).
